item.title: Re: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
The image "banjolele" could not be sourced and is assumed non-compliant. As such, it has since been removed.

As per the above source, the "bonifays" image is under public domain and thus compliant with CC 3.0

item.title: Provisional Licensing Information
item.content:encoded:
Note: Image source of "banjolele" is still unknown.

  • bonifays

Source: https://www.floridamemory.com/items/show/63107
License: Public domain

Title: Setting up exercises at Santa Rosa camp
Photographer: Herrington, G. L.
Taken in: 1920

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry that whoever wrote the Bonifay mythos has a deleted account, because I absolutely love it. It gives me all sorts of strange, sad feelings, nostalgia, longing, hope, but somehow hopelessness, and contentedness, but at the same time, dread.

It's like the relief you feel when you reach out to take a helping hand that's being offered by an old friend, but as you take it you see that something's wrong, it's not the hand of a friend, it's too big and too strong and it's crushing your fingers but before you can scream you wake up, and you're fine, it was all just a dream.

Wasn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Late to the party, but the plural "phenomena" is used at least twice here in place of the correct singular of "phenomenon" and it's really bugging me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: God and NGB willing, I'll be there to help soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stay safe!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Came back to reread this tonight because the tension preceding Hurricane Irma is almost unbearable, right now, and we aren't due for landfall until Sunday.

I originally wrote this entry and tied it back to the others after reading about the Okeechobee Hurricane in 1928. It killed more than 4,000 people, at a time that— compared to now— the state was barely populated. I was sitting at work tonight, talking to a coworker, looking at the sunny sky fade into a sunset— and we were just flabbergasted trying to contemplate how shocking, how act-of-God even a smaller hurricane must have been to people without our modern weather forecasting. Tomorrow's due to be sunny, too.

What struck me most about the 1928 hurricane, even before I became aware of the horrifying role racism place in the aftermath, were few memorials and records of the hundreds upon hundreds of anonymous poor, of migrant farm workers, of black Floridians amongst the dead. That's what this whole Bonifay series was about, trivial and over-written as it is. I couldn't shake that sense of melancholy, that thought of haunted waters.

I'm with my family on the eastern coast of South Fla, and I'm sure other SCP-ers live around the area, too. Please stay safe, and be kind.

item.title: Re: NB
item.content:encoded:
These intros are always more pretentious than I remember them being…

I wrote the above about three years ago now, and I no longer have a WL author page because I deleted my WL content. The link would have taken you to a long character bio with lots of fake academic citations and a description of a Florida triune goddess-styled deity. There were archival Florida photos of three unknown women, and emphasis on their contribution of stories about the unknown or unmourned dead.

I'm really only replying because I didn't know this was on TV Tropes and that's kinda cool.

Seriously, though, just Google "Florida 1928". The second article that comes up is almost too overt an explanation of what I was trying to do with these stories.

item.title: Re: NB
item.content:encoded: Library articles are deleted and I still don't know what this has to do with a hurricane like TV Tropes claims it to be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The concept is interesting but it lacks a satisfying payoff in my opinion. It doesn't give quite enough to the reader to get them speculating and in that case, I feel the addenda don't add much and could have been made shorter and still get the point across.

I do appreciate the fact that the author is not afraid of using cross-testing of SCPs. I get why many people would rather avoid it but it feels like something the SCP Foundation would absolutely do so to avoid it so much always seems odd to me. That said, I do believe that in this case it relies a bit too heavily on the reader being familiar with the other SPCs. Still, nice to see someone try it, at least.

All in all, neutral vote for me. Feels like it has the potential to be good but it's not quite there yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought for sure i'd commented on this series when i first read it; apparently not.

I love all 3 of these. They're simultaneously tragic and eerie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, it's always nice to hear that someone enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this. Like some others, it unsettled me as I read it, and uncovered more about the interconnected history and effects of these objects. You took a gamble in telling a story this way, and for me it paid off. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So…
…who are the Bonifays?
Are they one or many?

Or are they everyone?Everyone is Rotondo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is very underrated, more people need to read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That gave me chills…I'm not sure why, but it did.

+1

item.title: Re: NB
item.content:encoded: This one is kind of… meh. It really isn't as scary or interesting as a lot of other SCPs, and I think that you could have made a better addition to the Bonifay series of articles. At least the grammar is acceptable. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is almost a tale in article format, building towards something and telling a story via testing of related objects.

That was my goal from the first article— I came up with a story first (Okeechobee, 1928) then found the images, and then picked three objects to bring across the different tones/ideas. I probably wouldn't have posted any one of them as a standalone SCP, even SCP-1896, which was the best received.

That's not how I usually write articles, and I figured it would be a risky-but-interesting thing to try.

(There are at least two tales that will round if off a little and make the objects' origin more explicit, but I'm not sure whether they'll fit better here or at the Library.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, this barely stands on its own without the links to the other Bonifay artifacts. Not to mention I was ready to downvote before I saw "second effect" because that first effect is pretty basic stuff.

However, by the end of the addenda, I was feeling a little unsettled, as someone else had said. This is almost a tale in article format, building towards something and telling a story via testing of related objects.

Also thinking about something something Agent Banjolele

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very happy with the cross-testing here- it carries the article in a big way. I imagine some headcanons won't tolerate the in-universe combination of such obviously related items, but I dare say it works here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You learn something new every day — I had no idea there was such a thing as a banjolele. This SCP is vaguely unsettling and has done nothing to dissuade me from thinking Florida is full of weirdos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I started the draft back in December, and I cut a lot of content out to make it readable (including a lot more experiment log material in Addendum 1, before the cross-testing)— possibly too much, in retrospect. I'll let it sit for a few hours and see what revisions I can make.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep.

edit: That is to say, they all have the common theme of altering memory; none of the others alter identity to the same extent or alter documents at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the Bonifay mythos when you first started it, and I feel that this is a good addition. My only concern is that, without the cross-testing (which is excellent, by the way), this seems kind of thin. Upvote, but I'd like to see the banjolele itself fleshed out a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that what the rest of the Bonifay objects do as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It affects memory, identity, and alters documents and photographs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, add in the fact that it doesn't actually make sounds when compulsively played and they won't stop playing until someone grabs it from them. Then also add in the thing where it plays an unrememberable song and sings when it's alone, as well as being a Bonifay thing. Did I miss anything?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All it seems to do it make people play it.

Well, I understand "this wasn't interesting", but that's ^ not really an accurate statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Dem banjolele's unnaturall, I'm tellin' ya."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel unsettled by this, and I don't really know why. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not really interested in this. All it seems to do it make people play it. While I'd like to see the continuation of the Bonifay family, I feel like this is pretty weak. -1

item.title: NB
item.content:encoded:
Suggested reading order: SCP-1890, SCP-1896, SCP-1928.

This one was a long time in writing, and the other two have now been significantly rewritten for maximum cohesiveness. I'd especially appreciate a re-read of SCP-1890 as a part of a whole.

The music: sprechstimme, Dock Boggs, a banjolele sounds like a banjo on helium, the waters of Babylon inspired this SCP, and Americans have some great songs about death.
